title: The Deterrence Effects of Tax Whistleblower Laws: Evidence from New York’s False Claims Acts
abstract: In this study, we provide evidence on the effects of state tax whistleblower laws. We exploit a novel 2010 amendment to New York’s False Claims Acts (FCA) that explicitly extended whistleblower incentives to corporate income tax whistleblowers. We identify treated firms (firms exposed to New York’s FCA) using establishment-level data and descriptive analyses. Using a sample of firms exposed to New York and neighboring states, we find evidence that New York’s FCA reduced state tax avoidance. In cross-sectional tests, we find that effects are increasing in firms that grant fewer employee stock options and industry regulation, consistent with deterrence increasing in employee and regulator monitoring. We also find evidence that New York’s FCA deterred federal tax avoidance, consistent with positive vertical tax externalities. Next, we focus on particular tax strategies and find evidence of a reduced probability of Double Irish tax structures, reduced relationships to tax planning banks, reduced use of special purpose vehicles, and reduced outbound tax-motivated income shifting. We also find evidence that firms with the lowest (highest) cost of relocation (1) reduced (did not change) establishment counts in New York but (2) did not change (reduced) state tax avoidance. Finally, we disentangle general ex ante deterrence from ex ante peer deterrence using hand-collected New York tax whistleblower press releases from the Attorney General. We find evidence of both types of deterrence. Overall, this study provides policy-relevant evidence on the deterrence effects of tax whistleblower laws.

article.1. Introduction: Whistleblower laws are a growing and popular policy tool that incentivizes whistleblowing actions while also protecting potential whistleblowers (especially employees) from retaliation. Successful whistleblower actions result in substantial revenue to the government (e.g., fines or back taxes), attract media attention, and are touted by regulators (e.g.,Jones 2014,New York Attorney General’s Office 2018). However, their major benefit is often less obvious. Whistleblower laws can theoretically deter illegal behavior by outsourcing monitoring to potential whistleblowers (Ventry 2017). In the tax context, whistleblower laws can deter corporate tax avoidance by turning citizens and corporate insiders into “pseudo-revenue agents” that monitor tax fraud and increase the probability that misconduct is detected (Ventry 2014, p. 453). Based on this reasoning, several commentators have proposed state tax whistleblower laws as a way to reduce the “tax gap” (i.e., the difference between taxes legally owed and the taxes collected) (Hamilton 2014,Gould 2018).1However, the potential deterrence benefits of tax whistleblower laws are unclear. In this study, we document these deterrence benefits. In addition to examining firms’ state tax avoidance after whistleblower law adoption, we explore two other novel, policy-relevant externalities. First, state tax whistleblower laws may decrease federal tax avoidance because many states, such as New York, share a corporate tax base with the federal government (Walczak 2019). Thus, these state laws may deter federal tax avoidance. Second, whistleblowing enforcement events may deter industry peers’ tax avoidance. Industry peers often exploit similar tax avoidance strategies (Balakrishnan et al. 2019). Thus, enforcement against one firm may deter the tax avoidance of peers. Importantly, both effects may generalize to nontax settings. For example, the vertical enforcement externality may apply to securities fraud because both state and federal laws govern security regulations. Thus, state enforcement may deter federal securities fraud. Similarly, nontax whistleblowers in an industry may deter misbehavior by other firms in that industry. We use the passage of a novel amendment to New York’s False Claims Act (FCA) in 2010 as our setting. The amendment explicitly extended the scope of New York’s general FCA to income tax whistleblowers (Schneiderman 2010). By contrast, most state whistleblower laws specifically exclude income tax whistleblowers (Gould 2018).2The amendment was part of broader legislation that strengthened New York’s 2007 Medicaid-related FCA law (Schneiderman 2010). The tax amendment was included as a further potential punishment to government contractors engaging in fraud. Following its passage, however, the amendment has been used to target both contractor and noncontractor companies, leading to headline-grabbing tax fraud recoveries. For example, Sprint Co., Credit Suisse, and Bank of New York Mellon, among others, have all settled tax fraud cases arising from whistleblower actions under New York’s FCA (New York Attorney General’s Office 2018,Ventry 2019). Theoretically, tax whistleblower laws may deter corporate tax avoidance by increasing firm monitoring. These laws incentivize stakeholders (both internal and external) to increase monitoring by offering potential compensation to all whistleblowers and protection to internal stakeholders (i.e., employees). For example,Bereskin et al. (2019)provide experimental evidence that personal financial gain is a strong motivation for employee whistleblowers. New York’s FCA offers 15%–30% of the total recovered back taxes, fines, and penalties to the whistleblower, resulting in millions or even tens of millions of dollars awarded to successful whistleblowers (Hertel 2013,Jones 2014). To identify firms exposed (i.e., those with “nexus” and apportion substantial taxable income to New York) to the New York FCA, we identify the minimum proportion of firm establishments in New York required for a firm’s state income tax expense to exhibit sensitivity to New York’s corporate income tax rate.3We find that firms with more than 5% of their total establishments in New York report state effective tax rates (ETRs) that are sensitive to New York corporate tax rates. This sample of firms comprises our exposed group. Our nonexposed group is composed of firms that meet the same requirement for states that geographically neighbor New York (i.e., firms with more than 5% of their total establishments in Connecticut, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Massachusetts, or Vermont). We use a neighboring state sample to mitigate endogeneity concerns; by using neighboring states, we capture common political or geographic trends. Our sample spans from 2005 to 2015. Using a traditional difference-in-differences (DiD) design, we find evidence that the New York FCA deters state corporate tax avoidance. These results are consistent with tax whistleblowers generating deterrence effects, as intended. Next, we examine how internal and external stakeholder monitoring moderates the effects of the New York FCA. We find results consistent with FCA effects increasing in firms that grant fewer rank-and-file stock options. Employees with stock options are typically more aligned with shareholder interests and sensitive to firm profits compared with other employees (e.g.,Aldatmaz et al. 2018). Thus, they have a diminished incentive to monitor the firm and “blow the whistle” compared with other employees. Moreover, firms often discourage employee whistleblowing by granting employees stock options (Call et al. 2016). As a result, firms that grant fewer rank-and-file stock options are exposed to a greater threat of employee whistleblowing compared with other firms. Second, we find results consistent with the New York FCA’s effects increasing in industry regulation. Industry regulators are a credible whistleblower threat (Dyck et al. 2010,Huang et al. 2023). Thus, firms in regulated industries reduce their state tax avoidance more than other firms. Next, we provide novel evidence that tax whistleblower laws yield externalities to other governments (Keen 1998). State corporate tax bases are often similar or identical to the federal tax base. Therefore, state tax changes may affect federal tax collections and vice versa. For example,Dahlby and Wilson (2003)provide evidence that increases in the state income tax rate can increase federal tax revenues, whereasReingewertz (2018)finds evidence that changes in the federal tax rate decreases state tax revenue collections. Anecdotally, an article in theJournal of Multistate Taxationby state tax lawyers states that “Suits to date have targeted classic state tax issues … as well as federal income tax issues that also, because of federal conformity, are reflected on New York income tax returns” (Reed 2017, p. 5). Consistent with this intuition, we find evidence that the New York FCA reduced federal tax avoidance (i.e., a vertical tax enforcement externality). Importantly, this evidence should generalize to nontax settings. For example, state and federal governments both share jurisdiction over security regulations. Thus, state enforcement may deter federal securities fraud. Next, we examine specific tax strategies that may be affected by New York’s FCA. In particular, we focus on strategies that should reduce both state and federal tax payments. First, we find evidence that FCAs reduce U.S. outbound tax-motivated income shifting.4Outbound income shifting often exploits ambiguity in tax laws or transfer pricing differences. Thus, some facets of income shifting may be plausibly illegal and are ripe for whistleblowing. These results are consistent with anecdotal evidence. For example, the New York Attorney General (AG) office has pursued transfer pricing claims based on whistleblower reports against numerous companies, such as the Vanguard Group and Egon Zehnder, an international head-hunting company (JD Supra 2015,State of New York et al. v. Egon Zehnder International, Inc. et al. 2021). Second, we find evidence that the New York FCA decreases the probability of “Double Irish with a Dutch Sandwich” (Double Irish, hereafter) tax structure use.5The Double Irish tax structure was a popular method to reduce U.S. state and federal taxes on foreign earnings and domestic earnings booked overseas in the 2000s and 2010s (Kleinbard 2012,Gamage and Shanske 2018). This structure avoids triggering U.S. subpart F income and defers New York state and federal taxation of foreign earnings until the U.S. parent receives dividend payments (Walczak 2019). Commentators have suggested that these structures may yield whistleblowing opportunities (Terhufen 2015). Thus, our evidence is consistent with firms reducing their likelihood of using Double Irish structures because of an increased whistleblowing threat. Third, we find evidence that exposed firms reduce their use of special purpose entities (SPEs) following the New York FCA.Drucker (2007)andDemeré et al. (2020)find evidence suggesting that SPEs are often used to avoid state and federal taxes. Firms can use SPEs to shift New York income to low-tax jurisdictions. Given that some of these SPE-facilitated income-shifting strategies could be illegal, they may be targeted by whistleblowers. Fourth, we find that firms reduce the number of relationships with tax planning New York banks following exposure to the New York FCA. Banks facilitate client firm tax avoidance by designing, promoting, and facilitating tax planning strategies (Gallemore et al. 2019). Anecdotally, bank-facilitated tax avoidance has generated substantial litigation and ultimately, has generated fines and penalties (e.g.,DeCambre 2014,Goncalves 2018,Gallemore et al. 2019,Sebag 2021). Moreover, the New York FCA penalties specifically apply to persons conspiring to commit tax fraud, including tax planning intermediaries, such as banks and the tax-paying firm. Therefore, bank-facilitated tax avoidance is likely susceptible to whistleblowing, consistent with our results. Next, we examine whether firms shift activities out of New York in response to the New York FCA. Firms with low levels of activity in New York activity may reduce these activities rather than face increased tax whistleblower risk following the passage of the New York FCA.6However, these firms may also face smaller tax liabilities, thus reducing incentives to respond to the FCA. Thus, it is an empirical question as to whether firms with low New York exposure shift activities out of New York following the New York FCA’s passage. We find that firms with low New York activity levels pre-FCA reduce their New York establishments post-FCA but do not significantly alter their tax avoidance. These results carry implications for policymakers. Although the direct administrative costs of whistleblower programs are arguably limited, our results imply that firms may reduce economic activity in the jurisdiction because of a tax whistleblower law. Thus, these laws may lead to substantial costs for firms because of the decline in economic activity. By contrast, firms with a large presence in New York before the FCA reduce their tax avoidance but do not alter the number of their New York establishments, indicating the FCA deterred aggressive tax strategies among these firms. Next, we examine whether our main results are attributable to (1) general ex ante deterrence related to an increased whistleblowing threat from the law’s passage or (2) ex ante peer deterrence resulting from actual whistleblower actions under the new law against industry peers but not the firm itself. In other words, we examine whether increased detection risk because of the passage of the law or because of actual successful whistleblower litigation against industry peers explains the FCA’s deterrence effect. We collect the identity of whistle-blown firms from the press release archives of the New York State Office of the Attorney General during our sample period. We use these press releases as staggered treatment events for firms in the same industry as the targeted firm. Using a stacked cohort design, we find evidence that these press releases reduce the state tax avoidance of industry peers, consistent with ex ante peer deterrence. Next, we include press release indicators as additional variables in our main design and “horse race” the passage of New York’s FCA against the press release indicators. We find that the general deterrence effect from the New York FCA is incremental to the ex ante peer deterrence effect, but both remain significant In addition, we find evidence that New York’s FCA increased corporate tax revenues by $281 million or 7.7% per year. We perform several robustness tests. First, we use staggered state adoptions of Medicaid FCAs as placebo treatments in falsification tests.7We find no evidence that Medicaid FCA adoption affected corporate income tax avoidance in exposed firms compared with neighboring state firms. Second, we plot pretreatment parallel trends and find no evidence of divergent pretreatment trends between exposed and nonexposed firms. Third, we find that our results are robust to entropy balancing and propensity score matching to reduce observable differences between exposed and nonexposed groups. Our study provides novel evidence to the whistleblowing literature. Our study particularly complementsWilde (2017)andBerger and Lee (2022).Wilde (2017)finds evidence that firms reported to the U.S. Department of Labor for retaliation against financial reporting-related whistleblowers reduce their earnings management and tax avoidance. His results are consistent with deterrence at firms targeted by the whistleblower. Our study complements his findings by providing evidence that whistleblower laws yield (1) deterrence at targeted firms’ industry peers and (2) deterrence at firms exposed to whistleblower laws (prior to an actual whistleblower event).8Our study also reinforces the general findings inBerger and Lee (2022)but with a focus on tax.Berger and Lee (2022)find evidence that the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission’s whistleblower program (created by the Dodd–Frank Act) in conjunction with state FCAs deterred accounting fraud. We find similar deterrence benefits in a tax context while also documenting externalities, peer deterrence effects, and activity shifting. Our study also extends the literature on tax avoidance monitoring by firm stakeholders. For example, prior studies examine the effects of shareholder monitoring, sell-side analyst monitoring, and public scrutiny on corporate tax avoidance (Dyreng et al. 2016;Khan et al. 2017; andChen et al. 2018,2019a,b, respectively). In economics, several studies find that employer reporting of employee tax information results in decreased individual tax avoidance (e.g.,Kleven et al. 2011). We extend this literature by documenting the effects of increased monitoring incentives via whistleblower laws on corporate tax avoidance. Finally, we contribute to the literature on crossjurisdictional tax policy effects. For example, prior studies find evidence of vertical tax rate externalities; tax rate changes at the federal level affect state-level tax revenues (Besley and Rosen 1998,Esteller-Moré and Solé-Ollé 2001). Other studies find evidence that tax rates in one country affect tax collections in others (“horizontal externalities”). Our study extends this literature by documenting externalities to the federal government resulting from state-level tax enforcement policies.
article.2. Institutional Details and Theoretical: 2.1. Institutional Details Whistleblower laws are generally intended to deter illegal or fraudulent activities (Broderick 2007,Schneiderman 2010). State FCAs are the primary whistleblower law for nearly every state (Broderick 2007).9FCAs allow whistleblowers to litigate against the alleged criminal. Whistleblowers “step into the shoes of the government” (“qui tam” litigation) (Hertel 2013, p. 1897;Jones 2014). Anonymity is preserved. State attorneys general can join whistleblowers as plaintiffs. These AGs review the evidence in deciding to join, and in practice, they join when the probability of success is high. In such cases, the whistleblower defers to the AG in litigation; thus, the whistleblower “takes a back seat.” Regardless of whether the whistleblower or AG heads the litigation, the whistleblower is entitled to her or his portion of the recovery (Gilles and Friedman 2019). FCAs are often motivated by Medicaid fraud deterrence and explicitly preclude tax whistleblowers (Hertel 2013). However, five state FCAs do not include a corporate tax exclusion, although it is ambiguous as to whether they would apply to tax whistleblowers (Jones 2014). In our searches, we find no anecdotal evidence or legal filings indicating that tax whistleblowing is common in states other than New York. As of 2022, three state FCAs explicitly include tax whistleblower incentives and protections, but only New York (2010) and Washington, DC (2021) cover income tax whistleblowers (Williams et al. 2021). Thus, we focus on New York’s FCA. New York’s FCA awards whistleblowers up to 30% of the total delinquent tax recovery (the New York False Claims Act). Although it covers “fraudulent intent,” New York’s FCA also allows recovery for nonfraudulent tax avoidance. The burden of proof under the New York FCA requires that the “qui tam” litigator or the AG show the firm/defendant took a tax position with any of (1) intention to defraud or (2) reckless disregard of, (3) actual knowledge of, or (4) deliberate ignorance of the falsity of the position (the New York False Claims Act). Contrastingly, traditional tax fraud litigation requires only the highest burden of proof. Specifically, tax fraud convictions require evidence that the taxpayer acted only with intent to defraud. Under the FCA’s evidentiary standard, “a person can be held responsible for a false claim and required to pay treble damages even if he did not actually know that the claim was false and even if he were not deliberately trying to defraud the government” (Noonan and Comiskey 2011). Thus, “the reasonableness of a position simply does not matter” as reasonable tax positions may be penalized if the government has a different interpretation and the taxpayer took the position anyway (Kranz et al. 2022). Even if they succeed, such taxpayers will have to pay for costly litigation. Thus, firms with reasonable, innocuous tax positions may simply prefer to reduce tax avoidance, especially if the government disagrees with their position. The New York FCA’s evidentiary standard has been the subject of substantial controversy.10 In addition, New York’s FCA protects employees who blow the whistle from retaliation. Employers in New York are liable for special damages and double back pay if an employee whistleblower is “discharged, demoted, suspended, threatened, harassed or in any other manner discriminated against …” for whistleblowing by an employer or prospective employer (the New York FCA). Three pieces of anecdotal evidence suggest that managers are likely aware of the law within a year of its adoption. First, managers are likely aware of the law because the New York AG publicized the law aggressively shortly after its passage. The AG “rolled out its law with some fanfare” and labeled the law “a whistleblower law on steroids” (Breidenbach 2011,Rillotta and Herbert 2022). The AG “went public with his campaign against false claims” by hosting a conference at New York University in June 2011 dedicated to the new tax whistleblower law (Rillotta and Herbert 2022; seeCorporate Crime Reporter 2011for details about the conference). Moreover, numerous practitioners discussed the law soon after its passage, which further increased publicity (e.g.,Wetzler 2011). Second, tax professionals state they were informing clients about the law shortly after passage. For example, a 2010Tax Notesarticle states that at least one tax lawyer in New York planned to “talk to taxpayers and tax practitioners about New York’s … False Claims Act” (Hamilton 2010). A January 2011 article in theSyracuse Post-Standardmentions that “accountants and lawyers are notifying clients about the new law” (Breidenbach 2011). In a 2011 article, two tax lawyers note that “entities, individuals, and other governments will be watching this effort…” in reference to the New York Tax FCA (Southwell et al. 2011). Third, in 2012, the New York AG investigated the private equity industry’s tax avoidance based on a whistleblower tip in a highly publicized investigation. The investigation was covered in theNew York Times, theWall Street Journal, and other major news outlets (Albergotti et al. 2012,Confessore et al. 2012, andFreifeld and Roumeliotis 2012, respectively). These media outlets discuss the New York FCA’s tax amendment. We expect the media coverage to enhance managers’ awareness of the FCA. Overall, those involved with corporate tax planning (i.e., managers and tax professionals) were likely aware of the law soon after passage. Anecdotal evidence suggests that New York’s FCA has been effective against large, reputable firms. For example, a New York whistleblower received a multimillion-dollar recovery against Moody’s Corporation (Coppola 2020).11Sprint ($300 million) and Credit Suisse ($15 million) have paid large settlements for tax fraud to New York’s AG for whistleblower actions arising under New York’s FCA (New York Attorney General’s Office 2018,Berg & Androphy 2019). In fact, anecdotal evidence suggests that New York’s FCA has created excessive whistleblowing, leading to frivolous, but costly, litigation against large firms (Dobay and Do 2020,Friedman and Ahmadi 2022,NYSBA 2022).12 In non-FCA states, “large business interests” have opposed tax-inclusive FCA passage (Ventry 2019,Bologna 2020). For example, the California Chamber of Commerce wrote letters to the California legislature opposing passage of California’s proposed tax-inclusive FCA. The chamber’s board members include executives at the Walt Disney Corporation, Comcast, and Boeing, among other large companies. 2.2. Theoretical Framework We expect whistleblower laws to deter aggressive corporate tax avoidance. In the spirit ofBecker (1968)andAllingham and Sandmo (1972), we assume that firms weigh the marginal benefits of aggressive behavior against its marginal costs. The marginal costs of aggressive behavior are the potential fines, penalties, reputation, and other costs that firms face if caught engaging in aggressive behavior weighted by the probability of detection. Damages are large under the New York FCA to ensure that the whistleblower is paid while also ensuring that the government recovers lost tax revenues (Wetzler 2011). For example, the largest explicit cost is treble damages (i.e., 300% of the total recovery). The total recovery includes fines, interest, penalties, and back taxes. Interest amounts to approximately 47% of the unpaid liability if the amount is paid four years after it was owed (based on the calculator available on the New York Department of Taxation and Finance’s website). We believe 4 years is a reasonable number because the statute of limitations is 10 years, and FCA cases seem to take 3–5 years according to anecdotes (Fox 2016). In addition, taxpayers who avoid taxes with knowledge of illegality or reckless disregard to the legality of the tax avoidance owe penalties of 40% of the unpaid tax. Firms must also pay for successful whistleblowers’ attorney’s fees and legal costs (i.e., “fee shifting”). Courts have ruled that settlement generally counts as success, requiring fee shifting (Martire and Ferrante 2013). Attorneys’ fees are roughly 33.33% of the plaintiff’s total recovery (New York City Bar 2024). Legal costs are often 10% of attorneys’ fees (i.e., 3.33% of the plaintiff’s recovery). Thus, attorneys’ fees and legal costs amount to 36% of the plaintiff’s recovery. The New York FCA clearly states that these amounts are added to the treble damages (Dobay and Do 2020). We estimate that the total cost that firms face for engaging in tax avoidance ranges from 8 to 14 times the amount of taxes avoided (seeAppendix B). Moreover, if allegations are made public, the firm faces reputation costs even if the claims lack merit. For example, a tax lawyer states that “the second the FCA suit is filed by the Attorney General, there’s a press release that goes out and it effectively says XYZ Company is a tax fraud and we’re suing them for recovery. … It’s one thing to defend against a tax discrepancy. Now, you’re also looking at a public relations issue” (Dobay and Do 2020). Reputation costs can be substantial.Graham et al. (2014)survey tax executives and find that nearly 70% respond that potential harm to their firm’s reputation is “very important” or “important” when deciding what tax planning strategies to implement. Prior studies find evidence that reputation costs deter future tax avoidance, resulting in 13.3% less tax avoidance (Dyreng et al. 2016); may result in lower stock prices to some degree (Hanlon and Slemrod 2009); and reduce employee perceptions of their employers (Lee et al. 2021). Thus, we expect firms to reduce tax avoidance following the FCA because of the substantial explicit and reputation costs. InAppendix B, we provide a more formal analysis of the cost-benefit analysis for firms facing New York’s FCA using these institutional features. Moreover, we expect that whistleblower protections increase the probability of detection by increasing incentives for both internal and external stakeholders to engage in monitoring firms’ corporate tax avoidance. Employees often benefit from information advantages (Wilde 2017,Dube and Zhu 2021,Campbell and Shang 2022). Employees with incentives to exploit that information advantage because of whistleblowing rewards should substantially increase detection risk. For example,Butler et al. (2019)in an experimental setting document evidence that financial incentives play a primary role in motivating employee whistleblowing.Bereskin et al. (2019)find evidence that sociological motivations (in particular, prosocial motivations) for employee whistleblowers are associated with increased whistleblowing and Chief Executive Officer turnover. In nontax contexts, prior studies find that employees substantially alter firm behavior because of their information benefits. For example,Berger and Lee (2022)find evidence that the Dodd–Frank whistleblower program deterred accounting fraud, consistent with employees and other stakeholders effectively monitoring firms’ accounting functions because of whistleblower incentives. Relatedly,Wilde (2017)finds evidence that whistleblowers at particular firms reduce earnings management and corporate tax avoidance at those firms.Dube and Zhu (2021)find evidence that firms improve workplace practices following disclosure of employee reviews/information onGlassdoor.com. Similarly, external stakeholders often monitor tax avoidance. For example, prior studies find that stock liquidity is associated with lower aggressive corporate tax avoidance because liquidity increases shareholder monitoring (Chen et al. 2019a,b). Similarly, public pressure in the form of media coverage may deter corporate tax avoidance by increasing public scrutiny and monitoring of corporate tax avoidance (Dyreng et al. 2016). Industry regulators have been found to play a key role in blowing the whistle on financial fraud (Dyck et al. 2010). Anecdotally, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, the federal agency that regulates the transmission and wholesale sale of electricity, scrutinizes tax reporting, such as the accounting of investment tax credits to ensure that utility companies pass on the tax benefits to their customers (Deloitte 2018). Prior New York FCA whistleblowers have been both internal stakeholders (i.e., employees) and external stakeholders (i.e., external accountants, such as the Big 4), and they have included employees, consultants, and lawyers (Vincent 2013,Reed 2017,New York Attorney General’s Office 2021). Moreover, New York’s FCA is broad, and other stakeholders, such as industry regulators, are eligible as potential whistleblowers.13In sum, we expect that New York’s FCA (1) increases the detection risk of aggressive tax avoidance by increasing stakeholder monitoring and (2) increases the marginal cost of engaging in aggressive tax avoidance. As a result, we expect firms to respond by decreasing their aggressive corporate tax avoidance.
article.3. Setting, Data, and Research Design: We use a 2010 amendment to New York’s FCA that expanded coverage of the FCA to income tax whistleblowers as our setting. Under the amendment, firms with nexus to New York and that underpay taxes to the state of New York are subject to the FCA. Physical presence is sufficient to create nexus. However, even with nexus, firms may be unaffected by New York’s FCA if the taxable income they apportion to New York is economically small. Taxable income apportionment generally depends on the proportion of in-state sales, property, or labor relative to total sales, property, or labor. Thus, our measurement of firms exposed to New York is intended to identify firms with New York nexus and that apportion substantial amounts of income to New York. We use two descriptive analyses to identify firms with New York nexus and that apportion sufficiently large income to New York. First, we examine the distribution of firm establishments across all states. We find that, on average, 5.7% of firms’ establishments are exposed to a given state, indicating that they have nexus to the average state. Second, we identify the amount of firm activity in New York required for a firm’s state income tax expense to respond to New York corporate tax rates. Firms that apportion a substantial portion of their taxes to a state are likely to have state effective tax rates that are correlated with that state’s corporate tax rates. Therefore, they are likely to engage in tax planning (either innocuous or risky) in that state. Thus, they are likely to face increased risk when the FCA increases the threat of whistleblowing in that state. We partition this sample based on firms’ proportion of establishments in New York in the presample period between 1990 and 2004. Specifically, we divide firms’ proportion of establishments in New York into 5% intervals. Next, we regress firms’ state effective tax rates on New York’s corporate income tax rate for each subsample. We find that the 5%–10% interval is the first interval in which firms’ state effective tax rates are positively and significantly correlated with New York’s corporate income tax rates. This result suggests that firms with more than 5% of their establishments in New York report state effective tax rates that are responsive to New York income tax rates. Thus, we expect firms to respond to New York’s FCA and assign these firms to our treatment group. Our control group is composed of firms satisfying the same requirements for states neighboring New York (i.e., firms with more than 5% of their establishments in Connecticut, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Massachusetts, and Vermont). We obtain establishment-level data from the YourEconomy Time Series (YTS). YTS provides sales, employees, and establishments for publicly traded firms by state and year. We purchased the data set from Infogroup. Infogroup surveys establishments to obtain establishment-level data. They impute missing numbers using a proprietary formula. We obtain accounting data from Compustat. We obtain state-level economic variables from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. We obtain state tax avoidance legislation from various sources. Specifically, we supplement publicly available data sources (e.g., the Book of the States) with our own reading of corporate tax codes. We control for addback statutes, throwback statutes, sales factors, and combined reporting because these laws are often intended to reduce state corporate tax avoidance. Therefore, they may bias estimates if these laws were passed concurrently to the New York FCA. We discuss each of these below. We use state and industry-year fixed effects. We define state as the state where the firm has more than 5% of its establishment. We include firm-specific and state-specific covariates in our main specification. Standard errors are clustered at the state level. Our sample spans 2005–2015, five years before and five years after the 2010 amendment to New York’s FCA. This measure is truncated at zero and one. This measure likely captures innocuous and aggressive tax avoidance. This measure is intended to capture aggressive state corporate tax avoidance, which is likely undertaken with intent or “reckless disregard” and thus explicitly covered by New York’s FCA. Exposed firms have more than 5% of establishments in New York in yeart. We measure firms’ exposure to New York each year.15If a firm reduces its New York exposure to less than 5% of total establishments (either before or after the New York FCA), the firm is not classified as “exposed” at that point. If the same firm has more than 5% of establishments in Connecticut, Vermont, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, or Massachusetts, the firm is in the nonexposed group at that point. Otherwise, it drops out of the sample. Some observations pertain to firms with 5% or more establishments in New York and nonexposed states. Because our establishment data vary at the firm-state-year level but state effective tax rates do not, the New York observations of a firm are coded to one, whereas non-New York observations are coded to zero for the same firm.16 If effective, the New York FCA should reduce aggressive tax avoidance for firms operating in New York. Thus, we expect𝛽1β1to be positive. However, state tax plans are often multijurisdictional and lower tax burdens across multiple states. For example, Walmart’s captive Real Estate Investment Trust strategy generated tax deductions in at least 27 states. Such strategies were common during our sample period (McIntyre 2007). Deterrence of such strategies in New York may result in less tax avoidance across multiple states and thus, increases in state ETRs. The𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑡Postand𝑇𝑟𝑒𝑎𝑡Treatvariables are subsumed byIndustry××Year(represented by𝜌𝑘⁢𝑡ρk⁢tinEquation (1)) andState(represented by𝛾𝑠γsinEquation (1)) fixed effects, respectively. We includeIndustry××Yearfixed effects to control for possible unobserved time-varying industry shocks. We includeStatefixed effects to control for possible unobserved time-invariant state characteristics that might be correlated with the passage of the New York FCA and state tax avoidance. We include various firm-level covariates (Xit). First, we control for size using the natural log of sales (ln(Sales)). We control for size because large firms may benefit from more tax planning opportunities and resources or alternatively, might face higher political costs from tax planning compared with other firms (Zimmerman 1983). Next, we control for growth using the market-to-book ratio (MTB). Growth firms may pursue tax planning to save taxes and fund growth. We control for leverage (Lev) because it may substitute for tax planning (Graham and Tucker 2006). We control for research and development (R&D) because firms may try to use intangible assets to shift income to avoid taxes. We control for net operating losses (NOL) because firms may use them to reduce their tax bills. We control for capital expenditures (Capex) because they are tax favored and may reduce tax bills. We control for foreign income (FI) because multinational firms face a different set of tax planning opportunities than domestic firms. We control for return on assets (ROA) because tax avoidance may be increasing in profitability. We control for firms incorporated in Delaware because such firms may benefit from increased state tax planning opportunities (Dyreng et al. 2013). We control for extraordinary items because extraordinary items (Extraordinary) might affect state taxes (Laplante et al. 2018). All control variables exceptln(Sales)andMTBare scaled by average total assets. We also include several state-level covariates (Xst). These covariates capture state tax legislation that affects corporate tax avoidance. State-level policy presents the biggest threat to identification in our setting. If our sample states pass other tax avoidance legislation contemporaneous with New York’s FCA, our estimates will be biased Therefore, we include various state-level covariates. Moreover, these state-level covariates also capture other unobservable corporate tax laws that accompany each of these tax law changes. For example, states may pass observable laws that we control for along with other unobserved corporate tax laws to comprehensively address corporate tax avoidance. By controlling for observable tax legislation, we control for these other law changes. First, we control for the weight of sales factors (SalesFactor) in apportionment formulas.SalesFactoris the weight of sales in the apportionment formula to the state where the firm has establishments.Thrwbkcontrols for throwback laws that require firms to include income that is not subject to taxation in any other state in a particular state’s calculation of taxable income. This rule prevents firms from generating “nowhere income” that is not subject to taxation in any state because of nexus rules. We control for combined reporting laws (Combinedrep) that require firms to calculate taxable income as income across all related parties in the corporate entity. Taxes are then generally levied on the total corporate taxable income, which increases state tax revenues and decreases state corporate tax avoidance.Addbackcontrols for addback laws that require firms to “add back” Intellectual Property (IP)-related expenses or interest expenses paid to related parties in calculating taxable income. Thus, they effectively disallow deductions for certain related-party expenses.Appendix Aprovides detailed descriptions of our control variables. Importantly, in Table A5 in the Online Appendix, we find that these variables are not significantly correlated with the New York FCA.17Thus, these state corporate tax laws passed concurrently do not likely confound our analyses.

Cross-Sectional Tests 4.4.1. Rank-and-File Employee Stock Options. Next, we examine cross-sectional variation in stakeholder monitoring. First, we examine whether the New York FCA effects are increasing in firms that grant fewer employee stock options. We expect that firms that grant fewer employee stock options face a greater threat of employee whistleblowing. The costs of “blowing the whistle” are higher for employees with substantial stock option grants compared with other employees. Successful whistleblower litigation results in fines and penalties, reducing firm profits and thereby, reducing the value of stock options (e.g.,Carpenter et al. 2019). Moreover, employees with stock options are unlikely to blow the whistle prior to option vesting because unvested options may be forfeited if the employee leaves the firm, as is often the case after blowing the whistle (Aldatmaz et al. 2018). Therefore, we expect that the deterrence effects of New York’s FCA are decreasing in firm stock option grants to employees. FollowingCall et al. (2016)andHuang et al. (2020), we calculate the number of rank-and-file employee stock options as the total number of stock options minus the number of stock options owned by top executives, scaled by the number of shares outstanding. We followHuang et al. (2020)and control for an employee size effect in our calculation of the number of rank-and-file employee stock options. Specifically, we sort firm-years into quintiles based on the number of employees in each year, and within each quintile, we sort firm-years into quintiles based on the number of rank-and-file employee stock options in each year. We then pool firm-years with the same employee stock options quintile ranks across different employee size quintile ranks together. We use an indicator variableLowRFOptionsthat equals one if the firm’s number of employee stock options is in the lowest quintile rank and zero otherwise. We report our results in panel A ofTable 3. We find that the coefficients onPost××Treat××LowRFOptionsare positive and significant at conventional levels in columns (1) and (2) in panel A ofTable 3. Our results are consistent with deterrence effects increasing in employee monitoring effectiveness. We also find that the coefficients onPost××Treatare insignificant, consistent with high levels of employee stock options reducing whistleblowing incentives (Call et al. 2016).20 Table 3.Cross-Sectional Analyses Table 3.Cross-Sectional Analyses Notes. This table presents the results of two cross-sectional analyses. Next, we examine whether the New York FCA effects are increasing in industry regulation. We expect that firms in regulated industries face a greater threat of whistleblowing from industry regulators (Dyck et al. 2010,Huang et al. 2023). We classify the transportation (Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) 3700–3799), utilities (SIC 4900–4999), healthcare (SIC 5122), and other services (SIC 8000–8999) industries as regulated industries (Broughel and Francis 2017,Ampaabeng et al. 2022,Huang et al. 2023). The Effect of the New York FCA on Federal Tax Avoidance Next, we examine whether New York’s FCA “spilled over” onto federal corporate tax avoidance. Particularly, we examine whether New York’s FCA yields positive externalities to the federal government by deterring tax strategies that reduce both state and federal tax payments. Anecdotally, FCA whistleblowers in New York have targeted tax avoidance that reduces state tax avoidance but also federal tax avoidance because of New York’s conformity to the federal taxable income definition (Reed 2017). For example, a New York FCA whistleblower alleged that Citigroup benefitted from net operating losses that were invalid under the Internal Revenue Code and New York State Tax Law. Specifically, his complaint states that Citi should not have used NOLs to reduce its taxable income for purposes of the IRC or for that matter, the New York Tax Law (Reed 2017).21Given this anecdotal evidence and the costs of such cases for defendants, we expect that the New York FCA affects federal tax avoidance. We use two measures of federal corporate tax avoidance:FETRandadjFETR. We measure total federal ETR (FETR) as the total federal income tax expense divided by pretax income. This measure is truncated at zero and one. As with state corporate tax avoidance, we useFETRbecause the FCA may deter even innocuous federal corporate tax avoidance. We measure aggressive federal corporate tax avoidance as the industry-size-adjusted total federal ETR (adjFETR), followingBalakrishnan et al. (2019). We use these two measures in our main difference-in-differences design. InTable 4, we find that the coefficient onPost××Treatis positive and significant using either measure of federal corporate tax avoidance. These results provide evidence that New York’s FCA yields positive externalities to the federal government by deterring federal corporate tax avoidance. Our results carry implications for nontax contexts. Vertical externalities hinge on federal and state law sharing a common definition (Keen 1998,Gamage and Shanske 2018). In the tax context, New York’s definition of corporate taxable income is very similar to the federal definition (Walczak 2019). In nontax contexts, several state-level laws use definitions similar to federal laws. For example, the Business Roundtable lists several areas in which state and federal oversight overlaps (Business Roundtable 2019). First, state security laws (“blue sky laws”) are often identical or similar to federal securities laws.22Therefore, violations of state securities laws may imply violations of federal securities laws. Similarly, consumer protection laws often overlap. In fact, “the enforcement of consumer protection laws is partially shared between federal agencies … and state AG offices (Lynyak 2022).”23Thus, prosecutions for consumer protection law violations often involve both state and federal claims.24 4.6. Tax Planning Strategies Next, we examine the effects of New York’s FCA on specific tax strategies. We focus on tax strategies that reduce domestic taxable income and thereby, reduce both New York state and federal tax payments. First, we test whether the New York FCA deters firms from establishing relationships with tax planning banks. Anecdotal evidence suggests that banks often involve clients in aggressive international tax avoidance strategies. Tax authorities have successfully prosecuted clients and banks for these strategies, often with the help of whistleblowers (e.g.,ElBoghdady 2012,DeCambre 2014,Sears et al. 2021,Sebag 2021). We expect that our treated firms, which have major New York operations, rely on banks as tax avoidance intermediaries because New York is a major financial center.Gallemore et al. (2019)find that banks serve as tax planning intermediaries for their client firms, structuring, executing, and participating in tax planning transactions for their clients. Thus, we expect firms to reduce the number of relationships with tax planning New York banks following exposure to the New York FCA. FollowingGallemore et al. (2019), we measure bank-client relationships using loan contracts from DealScan and classify banks as tax planners if the average three-year cash ETR for all other clients of the firm’s bank is below the median client three-year cash ETR of all banks in at least three of the five years before the New York FCA years. We also require banks to have at least 10 other clients with nonmissing cash ETR values (Gallemore et al. 2019). Next, we limit tax planning banks to banks located in the state of New York because these banks are also exposed to the New York FCA. In this test, we replace the dependent variable inEquation (1)with ln(TaxBank), the natural logarithm of one plus the number of tax planning New York banks with which the firm has a relationship. The results in column (1) in panel A ofTable 5show that the coefficients onPost××Treatare negative and significant at conventional levels. These results show that the New York FCA reduces firm relationships with tax planning banks. Next, we focus on the FCA’s effects on the use of Double Irish structures (Harris and O’Brien 2018), SPEs (Demeré et al. 2020), and tax-motivated outbound income shifting (Klassen and Laplante 2012). Double Irish structures were a highly popular method to reduce U.S. taxes, including repatriation taxes, on profits earned in foreign jurisdictions. The Double Irish tax structure reduces both federal and New York taxable income because New York tax law follows the federal tax code in its treatment of subpart F income and foreign dividends. Although legal, these structures may elicit litigation because of undervaluing intellectual property (see, e.g.,Raymond and Bergin 2016). Similarly, SPEs are often used to facilitate tax-advantaged transactions (e.g., as part of an offshore tax sheltering strategy or as part of “captive” strategies) (Takavoli 2003). Consistent with SPEs yielding tax benefits,Demeré et al. (2020)find evidence that SPE use is positively associated with corporate tax avoidance. Tax-motivated outbound income shifting often exploits ambiguity in transfer pricing differences to reduce domestic taxable income and thereby, reduces both state and federal tax payments. Thus, some facets of income shifting may be plausibly illegal and are ripe for whistleblowing (Law 360 2015,State of New York et al. v. Egon Zehnder International, Inc. et al. 2021). Activity Shifting Responses. Next, we examine whether some firms shift activities out of New York to reduce exposure to the New York FCA. For some firms, shifting activities may be less costly than unwinding highly profitable tax plans. On the other hand, they may not owe large amounts of taxes in the first place because of limited activity in the state. In that case, the New York FCA may have no effect on these firms. Thus, whether firms with low New York exposure reduce activities following the FCA is an empirical question. First, we examine whether firms with a low ex ante New York presence reduce their New York operations following FCA passage. New York Attorney General Tax Whistleblower Press Releases. Next, we disentangle deterrence effects between general ex ante deterrence and ex ante peer deterrence. Whistleblower laws are intended to offer two deterrence benefits. As noted, whistleblower laws increase detection risk by incentivizing monitoring by potential whistleblowers (i.e., ex ante deterrence). In a complementary effect, firms may update their perceptions of detection risk or punishment magnitude following successful whistleblower actions against peers. Thus, we next examine whether our main deterrence results reflect general ex ante deterrence, ex ante peer deterrence, or both. Importantly, in this test, we control for ex post deterrence (i.e., firm reactions to whistleblowing events at the firm itself because targeted firms are omitted from our sample). We identify firms with peers that settled tax FCA litigation by collecting settlement press releases from the New York State Office of the Attorney General (tax whistleblower press releases or press releases hereafter). We next match each settlement firm to their industries using the French–Fama 12 industry classification. Our treated industries are finance (#11); healthcare, medical equipment and drugs (#10); wholesale, retail, and some services (#9); and telephone television transmission (#7). We use a stacked cohort design because treatment timing varies across industries and across time (Baker et al. 2022). We form three cohorts pertaining to our treated industries. In our first cohort,Post_ind××Treat_indequals one for firms in (1) wholesale, retail, and some services and (2) telephone television transmission in 2012 and onward. Each of these industries is affected by tax whistleblower settlement in 2012. In our second cohort,Post_ind××Treat_indis equal to one for firms in healthcare, medical equipment, and drugs in 2014 and onward. In our third cohort,Post_ind××Treat_indequals one for firms in finance in 2015 and thereafter. The industries in our second and third cohorts are affected by one tax whistleblower settlement in 2014 and 2015, respectively. For each cohort, we use “clean” controls (i.e., firms that are never treated because they are in untreated New York industries or are in neighboring states). We use raw state ETRs (rather than industry-size adjusted) becausePost_ind××Treat_indis industry specific. We restrict our sample to five years before the treatment year, but the postperiod varies because establishment data are only available until 2017. We includeCohort××YearandCohort××Statefixed effects. Results are reported in panel A ofTable 7, and we find that the coefficient onPost_ind××Treat_indis positive and significant. These results are consistent with firms reducing their state tax avoidance following industry peers’ tax whistleblower settlements. Thus, our results are consistent with ex ante peer deterrence effects from the New York FCA. State Tax Collections. Next, we examine whether state tax revenues respond to the New York FCA laws to estimate the magnitude of economic benefits to New York. We useCorpTax(state corporate tax revenues in billion dollars) and the natural log of this variable as dependent variables in columns (1) and (2) in panel C ofTable 7, respectively. As in our main model specification (Equation (1)), our variable of interest isPost××Treat. We control for state unemployment rate (Unemployment), gross state product growth (RealGSPGrowth), and the natural logarithm of the state’s population (ln(Population)). We include state and year fixed effects. We cluster standard errors by state. Our sample continues to span from 2005 to 2015. We report results in panel C ofTable 7. Across both columns, we find that the coefficient onPost××Treatis positive and significant at conventional levels. In terms of economic magnitude, we find that New York corporate tax revenue collections increase by $281 million or 7.7% per year following the passage of the New York FCA.31Taken together, our results are consistent with state tax whistleblower protections substantially increasing state corporate tax revenue collections by deterring state corporate tax avoidance. 4.8. Robustness Tests We perform several robustness tests that are reported in the Online Appendix. First, we conduct placebo tests with Medicaid FCAs to ensure that our results are not spurious. Medicaid FCAs target Medicaid fraud and have no direct impact on tax avoidance. We use the passage of Medicaid FCAs as treatment events. Firms in states passing Medicaid FCAs are our treatment group. Firms from states neighboring those with Medicaid FCAs serve as the control group. This approach expands the sample beyond our main sample, which only contains New York firms and neighboring state firms. Using Medicaid FCA passage as treatment events, we find no evidence that Medicaid FCAs influence state tax avoidance. Thus, the results suggest that our main findings are not spurious. Second, we examine a subset of firms operating solely within a single state (Connecticut, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Massachusetts, or Vermont). This significantly reduces our sample size, but our findings continue to hold, suggesting that our classification of firms as exposed and nonexposed does not bias our findings. In addition, we use two matching approaches to construct our sample: entropy balancing and propensity score matching. These methods ensure that the differences between the treatment and control groups do not skew our results. We match observations on the covariates from our main specification for both techniques. For added robustness, we include both firm-level and state-level control variables in our analysis. Our specification remains otherwise unchanged. We find results consistent with our main findings. Third, we adopt an alternative approach to identify firms’ exposure to the New York FCA. Specifically, we use total sales from establishment-level data instead of the number of New York establishments. We choose firms with at least 5% of their total sales in New York as our treatment group and compare them with similar firms in neighboring states. We find results consistent with our main findings. We also probe the robustness of our results to different exposure thresholds. Specifically, we classify firms as exposed when 20% or more of their establishments are in New York. We find that effects are larger using this alternative threshold, consistent with effects increasing as firms are more exposed to New York. Fourth, we explicitly address potential bias from firms classified as both exposed and unexposed to the FCA over different years. Firms that decrease exposure (increase exposure) may be classified as unexposed (exposed) in later years. Similarly, some firms may be both exposed and nonexposed in the same year. We take two approaches. First, we classify firms with 5% or more of their establishments in both New York and a neighboring state as exposed. This approach biases against finding any effect. Second, we simply drop firms that are exposed to New York and a neighboring state, which substantially reduces our sample. Our results are robust to using either approach. For additional robustness on treatment/control classification, we control for firms that operate in both New York and neighboring states in our main specification. First, we introduce a binary variable for firms with significant establishments in both New York and a neighboring state and find that including this variable does not alter our main findings. Second, we use a continuous variable to account for control firms with New York establishments and treatment firms with establishments outside New York. Specifically, we add a covariate that captures the proportion of New York establishments to total establishments. Our main results are nearly identical after including this covariate. Fifth, we use alternative tax avoidance variables. We examine uncertain tax benefits (UTBs) to assess tax planning risk. UTBs reflect managers’ views on uncertain state and federal tax positions (Rego and Wilson 2012,De Simone et al. 2020). We find evidence that UTBs decrease after the FCA. We also find evidence that the FCA increases both cash and Generally Accepted Accounting Principles ETRs. Finally, we assess the potential confounding effects of changes in New York’s corporate tax laws as well as corporate tax laws in the control states. Specifically, we analyze whether changes in the state corporate income tax rate, addback statutes, throwback statutes, combined reporting, or sales factor proportion coincide with the New York FCA’s passage. We use these law changes as dependent variables and FCA passage as our treatment variable. We do not find any evidence that the FCA is significantly associated with these law changes, suggesting limited potential for confounding.
article.5. Conclusion: In this paper, we provide evidence on the efficacy of tax whistleblower laws. We use New York’s 2010 False Claims Act, which expanded coverage of the False Claims Act to income tax laws, as our setting. In particular, we find evidence that the New York FCA’s 2010 whistleblower tax amendment reduced state corporate tax avoidance. We find that effects are decreasing in the amount of rank-and-file stock options awarded by a firm, consistent with a greater whistleblowing threat when employee incentives are less aligned with employers. We also find that effects are increasing in industry regulation, consistent with threats of regulators monitoring efforts increasing because of whistleblower incentives. In additional tests, we document evidence of vertical externalities; New York’s FCA reduced federal corporate tax avoidance. Moreover, we document the particular tax strategies that are deterred by the New York FCA, and we document some evidence of activity shifting out of New York among firms with low New York exposure. Moreover, we disentangle ex ante deterrence from ex ante peer effects using hand-collected tax whistleblower press releases. First, we find that firms reduce their state tax avoidance following press releases of industry peers’ tax whistleblower settlements, indicating ex ante peer deterrence effects. Second, we include both press releases and FCA passage as treatment variables in the same specification. We find evidence that both peer settlement industry press releases and FCA passage reduce state tax avoidance. Thus, our evidence is consistent with distinct ex ante peer deterrence effects and ex ante general deterrence effects. Our results contribute to the literature on whistleblowers by (1) documenting the deterrence benefits of whistleblower incentives on corporate tax avoidance, (2) documenting crossjurisdictional externalities from whistleblower laws, and (3) distinguishing ex ante deterrence effects from ex ante peer deterrence. Therefore, our results provide policy-relevant evidence on the efficacy of an increasingly important state tax tool: whistleblower laws. Moreover, the latter two effects generalize beyond tax avoidance settings and may thus inform nontax policymakers.
